startScreenOptions.title: AN22.1-AN22.7 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 4 embedded questions for AN22.1-AN22.7.

interactions.label: A patient develops rapid fluid accumulation in the pericardial cavity after a ch

params.question: A patient develops rapid fluid accumulation in the pericardial cavity after a chest injury. Why does this specifically impair cardiac function?

answers.text: The fluid obstructs the coronary arteries, causing myocardial ischaemia

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Pericardial fluid fills the space between the parietal and visceral layers of the serous pericardium, surrounding the heart externally. It does not enter or obstruct the coronary arteries. The impairment is mechanical — the compressed heart cannot fill with blood.

answers.text: The inelastic fibrous pericardium cannot expand, so the fluid compresses the heart and prevents adequate filling

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! The fibrous pericardium is made of dense connective tissue that cannot stretch. When fluid accumulates faster than the sac can accommodate, rising pressure compresses all four chambers, preventing adequate diastolic filling. This is cardiac tamponade, identified clinically by Beck's triad: hypotension, distended neck veins, and muffled heart sounds.

answers.text: The fluid disrupts the electrical conducting system within the heart wall

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The conducting system lies within the myocardium, not in the pericardial cavity. Tamponade impairs the heart mechanically by compressing its chambers, not by disrupting electrical conduction.

answers.text: The fluid enters the transverse sinus and compresses the aorta

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The transverse sinus is a passage behind the aorta and pulmonary trunk used during cardiac surgery. Tamponade involves generalised fluid accumulation throughout the pericardial cavity, compressing the heart chambers themselves, not isolated pressure on a single vessel.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the pericardium section. The fibrous pericardium cannot stretch. Rapid fluid accumulation compresses all four chambers, preventing filling. This is cardiac tamponade — Beck's triad (hypotension, distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds) identifies it clinically.

Well done. The inelastic fibrous pericardium is the key — rapid fluid accumulation has nowhere to go, so it compresses the heart. Emergency treatment is pericardiocentesis via a needle inserted below the xiphoid process.

interactions.label: The fibrous skeleton of the heart allows electrical impulses to pass freely betw

params.question: The fibrous skeleton of the heart allows electrical impulses to pass freely between the atrial and ventricular myocardium.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Incorrect. The fibrous skeleton actually blocks electrical conduction between atria and ventricles — it acts as an insulator. The only conducting pathway through it is the AV node and Bundle of His. Without this insulation, atria and ventricles would contract simultaneously instead of sequentially.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — that statement is false! The fibrous skeleton is an electrical insulator that completely separates the atrial and ventricular muscle masses. The only pathway for impulses to cross from atria to ventricles is through the atrioventricular node and the Bundle of His. This ensures the atria contract and finish emptying before the ventricles begin to contract.

metadata.title: The fibrous skeleton of the heart allows

interactions.label: Blockage of the left anterior descending artery is often called the 'widow-maker

params.question: Blockage of the left anterior descending artery is often called the 'widow-maker.' Which territory does this artery supply that explains why its occlusion is so dangerous?

answers.text: The lateral wall of the left ventricle and the left atrium

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The lateral wall of the left ventricle is primarily supplied by the left circumflex artery, not the LAD. Left circumflex occlusion causes a lateral myocardial infarction.

answers.text: The right ventricle and the sinoatrial node

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The right ventricle and the SA node are mainly supplied by the right coronary artery. RCA occlusion causes an inferior myocardial infarction and may produce heart block.

answers.text: The anterior wall of the left ventricle and the interventricular septum

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! The LAD runs down the front of the heart between the two ventricles, supplying the anterior wall of the left ventricle and the anterior two-thirds of the interventricular septum. Occlusion damages a large portion of the heart's main pumping chamber, making it the most dangerous pattern of myocardial infarction.

answers.text: The posterior wall of the left ventricle and the coronary sinus

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The posterior wall is supplied by the posterior descending artery, which in 85% of people arises from the right coronary artery. Posterior MI is the pattern most easily missed — posterior leads V7–V9 are needed to detect it.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the coronary artery territories. The LAD supplies the anterior wall of the left ventricle and the interventricular septum. Its occlusion causes an anterior MI — the most dangerous pattern because it damages a large portion of the heart's main pumping chamber.

Exactly right. The LAD supplies the largest territory of the left ventricle — the anterior wall and the septum. This is why its occlusion causes the most extensive and dangerous pattern of myocardial infarction.

metadata.title: Blockage of the left anterior descending

interactions.label: A transplanted heart, with all its nerve connections severed, continues to beat

params.question: A transplanted heart, with all its nerve connections severed, continues to beat in a regular rhythm. Why does this occur?

answers.text: The fibrous skeleton stores electrical energy that sustains ventricular contraction

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The fibrous skeleton is a structural and insulating framework made of dense connective tissue. It does not store or generate electrical energy — its role is to block impulses from crossing between atria and ventricles except through the AV node.

answers.text: Residual adrenaline in the bloodstream stimulates the heart muscle directly

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. While circulating adrenaline can modulate heart rate, it is not what initiates each heartbeat. The heart has its own intrinsic pacemaker that generates rhythmic impulses independently of hormonal or neural stimulation.

answers.text: The transplanted heart quickly develops new nerve connections from the recipient

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Cardiac nerve reinnervation after transplant is slow and incomplete. The heart beats immediately after transplantation because it has its own built-in pacemaker that requires no external neural input.

answers.text: The sinoatrial node generates electrical impulses intrinsically without requiring neural input

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! The SA node, located in the upper right atrium, is the heart's natural pacemaker. It consists of specialised cardiac muscle cells that fire at 60–100 beats per minute entirely on their own. This intrinsic automaticity is why a denervated transplanted heart maintains a steady rhythm from the moment it is implanted.

overallFeedback.feedback: Review the conducting system. The sinoatrial node in the upper right atrium is the heart's natural pacemaker. It generates electrical impulses at 60–100 bpm without any neural input — this is why a transplanted heart with severed nerves still beats rhythmically.

Well done. The SA node's intrinsic automaticity is what makes cardiac transplantation possible — the heart needs no brain connection to beat. The entire conducting system (SA node → AV node → Bundle of His → bundle branches → Purkinje fibres) is self-contained within the heart.
